course_info.title: Professional UX Practice
prerequisites: UX406 - UX Strategy and Business Impact

class_schedule.topic: The UX Profession: Roles, Career Paths, and Industry Landscape
learning_objectives: Map the landscape of UX roles: product design, UX research, interaction design, content design
Identify career progression paths from junior to principal/director
Understand how UX teams are structured in startups, agencies, and enterprises
readings: Org Design for Design Orgs by Merholz & Skinner, Ch. 1-3
The State of UX in 2024 (NNg annual report)

lecture_notes.introduction: The UX profession has matured significantly. Understanding the role landscape, career paths, and organizational structures prepares you to make strategic career decisions and navigate professional environments effectively.
key_concepts: UX role spectrum: generalist (product designer) to specialist (UX researcher, interaction designer, content strategist)
Career levels: junior/associate, mid-level, senior, staff/principal, director/VP
Team structures: centralized, embedded, hybrid design teams
Agency vs. in-house vs. freelance: trade-offs of each work model
The T-shaped designer: breadth across UX with depth in a specialty
lecture_notes.demonstration: Panel discussion with 3 UX professionals at different career stages sharing their career paths, daily work, and advice for new designers
setup_requirements: Industry salary and role data references

review.value: Broad competence across all UX disciplines (the horizontal bar of the T) with deep expertise in one area (the vertical bar). Employers value versatility with a specialty — not generalists who are shallow everywhere or specialists who cannot collaborate.

Embedded: designers sit within product teams (closer to the work, risk of isolation). Centralized: designers sit in a design department (stronger design community, risk of disconnection from product).

learning_objectives: Present design work persuasively to non-design stakeholders
Handle design feedback and pushback constructively
Write design proposals and project scopes
readings: Articulating Design Decisions by Tom Greever, Ch. 1-5
Design is a Job by Mike Monteiro, Ch. on Client Management

lecture_notes.introduction: The best design work fails if you cannot communicate its value, handle feedback, and manage expectations. Professional practice is as much about communication as about craft.
key_concepts: Framing design decisions in user needs and business outcomes
Handling 'I don't like it' feedback: separating subjective preference from usability
Design proposals: scoping work, estimating effort, defining deliverables
Setting expectations: what UX can and cannot solve
Conflict resolution: navigating disagreements between design, product, and engineering
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play exercise: presenting a design recommendation to a skeptical product manager, handling three rounds of pushback with data-backed responses

review.value: 'I don't like the blue color' is subjective. 'Users did not notice the CTA button — 3 of 5 test participants missed it' is objective. Redirect subjective feedback to objective criteria whenever possible.
Problem statement > Proposed approach > Timeline and milestones > Deliverables > Assumptions and risks > Investment. This structure works for internal proposals and client-facing scopes alike.

learning_objectives: Identify dark patterns and manipulative design techniques
Apply ethical frameworks to design decisions involving user data and persuasion
Navigate ethical dilemmas when business goals conflict with user welfare
readings: Ruined by Design by Mike Monteiro, Ch. 1-4
Deceptive Design (formerly Dark Patterns) by Harry Brignull

lecture_notes.introduction: Designers have power. Every interaction pattern, every default setting, every notification schedule shapes human behavior. With that power comes responsibility to advocate for user welfare, even when it conflicts with short-term business metrics.
key_concepts: Dark patterns: confirmshaming, roach motels, forced continuity, hidden costs, misdirection
Ethical design frameworks: do no harm, informed consent, transparency, user control
Attention economy ethics: notification design, infinite scroll, engagement metrics
Data ethics: what to collect, how to store, how to communicate, how to allow deletion
Ethical dilemmas: when business KPIs incentivize manipulative design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Dark pattern audit: class identifies and documents manipulative design patterns in 5 popular apps, categorizing by type and severity, then proposes ethical alternatives

review.value: Using guilt or shame to prevent users from opting out: 'No thanks, I don't care about my health.' This manipulative copy exploits emotion to override user choice. Ethical alternative: 'No thanks' without emotional manipulation.

Three questions for every design decision: (1) Would I be comfortable if the user fully understood what this design is doing? (2) Would I want this done to me? (3) Would I be proud if this decision were public?

learning_objectives: Set up a UX freelance practice: pricing, contracts, and client acquisition
Manage freelance projects: scoping, timelines, and client communication
Navigate common freelance challenges: scope creep, late payments, and difficult clients
readings: Design is a Job by Mike Monteiro, Ch. on Contracts and Money
The Business of UX (UX Mastery article collection)

lecture_notes.introduction: Many UX careers include freelance or consulting work. Understanding the business side — pricing, contracts, scope management, and client relationships — is essential for professional independence.
key_concepts: Pricing models: hourly, project-based, value-based, retainer
Contracts: scope of work, payment terms, revision limits, kill fee
Client acquisition: networking, referrals, portfolio marketing, platforms
Scope creep management: change orders, documentation, firm boundaries
The consultation process: discovery, proposal, execution, delivery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contract workshop: writing a real freelance contract for a UX project — defining scope, deliverables, revision rounds, payment milestones, and termination terms

review.value: Charging based on the value your work creates for the client, not the hours spent. If your checkout redesign will increase revenue by $500K, charging $50K is reasonable — regardless of whether it takes 40 hours or 200.

Define deliverables precisely in the contract. When clients request additions: 'That is a great idea. It is outside the current scope. I can add it as a change order for $X with a timeline extension of Y days.' Never absorb scope creep silently.

class_schedule.topic: Building a UX Career: Networking, Growth, and Leadership
learning_objectives: Build a professional network through conferences, communities, and mentorship
Plan continuous learning and skill development throughout your career
Develop leadership skills for senior and management roles
readings: The Making of a Manager by Julie Zhuo, Ch. 1-3
Resilient Management by Lara Hogan, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: A successful UX career is built over decades through continuous learning, intentional networking, and gradual expansion of influence. This session provides the strategic framework for long-term career growth.
key_concepts: Professional networking: conferences, local meetups, online communities, mentorship
Continuous learning: staying current with tools, methods, and industry trends
From individual contributor to manager: the career fork and how to decide
Design leadership: influence without authority, design culture, hiring
Personal brand: writing, speaking, teaching, and open-source contribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Career planning workshop: mapping a 5-year career plan with skills development milestones, networking targets, and leadership development goals

review.value: At senior level, careers fork: Individual Contributor (IC) path leads to Staff/Principal Designer (deep craft, high influence). Management path leads to Design Manager/Director (team building, organizational impact). Both are valid — choose based on what energizes you.
Senior designers lead through influence: establishing design principles, mentoring juniors, setting quality standards, and creating processes. You do not need a management title to lead.

questions.question: A product manager wants to add a pop-up that prevents users from closing their browser tab until they complete a sign-up form. As the UX designer, how would you respond?
questions.answer: Response: (1) Name the pattern: this is a 'roach motel' dark pattern — easy to get in, hard to get out. Browser exit prevention is hostile and manipulative. (2) Explain the business harm: users who are forced to complete sign-ups will have low engagement, high churn, and may leave negative reviews — the metric gain is illusory. (3) Provide an alternative: if the goal is reducing abandonment, address the root cause. Use exit-intent surveys to understand why users leave, simplify the form, or offer a save-for-later option. (4) Reference ethics and legality: in some jurisdictions (EU under consumer protection laws), preventing users from leaving a page may have legal implications. Frame this as both an ethical and business-risk concern.

rubric.key: Identifies the pattern as manipulative/dark pattern
Explains business harm beyond ethical concern
Proposes ethical alternative that addresses the real goal

questions.question: How would you handle a client who says 'Just make it look like the Apple website' as their design direction?
questions.answer: Response approach: (1) Acknowledge the aspiration without dismissing it: 'Apple's design is widely admired. Let us dig into what specifically appeals to you about their approach.' (2) Uncover the real need: ask what qualities they see — is it the minimalism, the typography, the photography quality, the whitespace? Different answers lead to different design directions. (3) Redirect to their users: 'Apple designs for consumer electronics shoppers. Your users are B2B procurement managers. The aesthetic can be clean and minimal, but the information architecture and interaction patterns must serve your users' needs, which are very different.' (4) Translate the feedback into actionable design principles: 'What I hear is: clean, spacious, high-quality imagery, and confident typography. We can apply these principles to your product while maintaining the functional requirements your users need.' Never dismiss client feedback — translate it into design requirements.

rubric.key: Probes for the underlying need behind the reference
Redirects to the client's actual users and context
Translates feedback into actionable design principles

questions.question: A freelance client requests a fourth round of revisions on a project scoped for two rounds. The contract includes two revision rounds. How do you handle this?
questions.answer: Steps: (1) Reference the contract professionally: 'Per our agreement, the project includes two rounds of revisions. We have completed both rounds.' (2) Offer a path forward: 'I am happy to do additional revision rounds. Each additional round is billed at $X, with an estimated timeline of Y days. Shall I prepare a change order?' (3) If the client pushes back, ask what is driving the additional revisions: unclear feedback in earlier rounds (your opportunity to improve the feedback process), changing requirements (scope creep that needs addressing), or genuine quality issues (your responsibility to fix within scope). (4) Maintain the relationship: be firm on boundaries but flexible on how to solve the problem. Documenting everything in writing protects both parties.

rubric.key: Offers a clear path forward with pricing
Investigates the root cause of additional revisions

questions.question: You are a senior UX designer considering whether to pursue a management path or continue as an individual contributor. What factors should inform this decision?
questions.answer: Factors: (1) What energizes you — do you get energy from mentoring people and building teams, or from deep craft work and solving complex design problems? (2) How you want to spend your time — managers spend 60%+ of time in meetings, 1:1s, hiring, and process; ICs spend 60%+ on design work, research, and prototyping. (3) Your influence model — managers influence through people and process; ICs influence through craft excellence and thought leadership. (4) Reversibility — management can be harder to reverse; you can try IC leadership (tech lead, design lead) without fully committing to management. (5) Your organization's IC ladder — some companies have strong Staff/Principal IC paths with equal compensation to management; others do not, making management the only advancement option. Neither path is superior — the right choice depends on your strengths, energy sources, and organizational context.

rubric.key: Identifies energy and daily work differences
Notes neither path is inherently superior

major_assignments.title: Professional Practice Capstone
major_assignments.description: Complete a professional practice capstone that demonstrates readiness for the UX profession. Deliver a polished portfolio, a freelance/consulting proposal for a real project, an ethics case study, and a 5-year career development plan.
objectives: Finalize a professional-grade portfolio ready for job applications
Write a professional proposal or scope of work for a real UX project
Analyze an ethical dilemma in UX design with a recommended resolution
Create a strategic career development plan
deliverables: Professional proposal or scope of work for a real UX project (5-8 pages)
Ethics case study: analysis of a real dark pattern with ethical redesign (3-5 pages)
5-year career development plan with milestones and skill targets
Professional presentation of one case study (15 minutes, recorded)

criteria: 3-4 compelling case studies with process and impact
Portfolio UX is responsive, accessible, and performant
Writing is professional and scannable
About page and resume are polished
Scope is well-defined with clear deliverables
Pricing and timeline are realistic and justified
Assumptions and risks are documented
Professional tone and format
Dark pattern correctly identified and categorized
Harm to users analyzed from multiple perspectives
Ethical redesign maintains business viability
Analysis demonstrates mature ethical reasoning
5-year vision is specific and realistic
Skill development milestones are actionable
Networking and leadership development planned
Plan accounts for industry trends and personal strengths
Professional portfolio ready for real job applications
Realistic and well-structured project proposal
Thoughtful ethical analysis with practical redesign
Strategic career plan with actionable milestones

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on professional practice, ethics, and career development

professional_exercises.description: Weekly exercises on communication, proposals, and ethics analysis

practice_capstone.description: Professional practice capstone with portfolio, proposal, ethics study, and career plan

industry_engagement.description: Attending UX events, conducting informational interviews, and documenting learnings

mock_interviews.description: Mock design interviews with industry professional feedback

course_policies.attendance: Guest speaker sessions and mock interviews are mandatory. More than 2 absences affect participation and mock interview grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Professional deliverables must be on time — deadlines are a professional skill. Late work penalized 15% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback on portfolios is required. All written deliverables must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All case studies must represent your real work. Inflating your role, fabricating metrics, or using AI-generated case study content without disclosure is professional dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Portfolio platform, presentation software, video recording for mock interviews, and professional networking platform profile (LinkedIn).
